definition: Wonderful, excellent, or very good. A universal term of approval.
example: That new picture at the drive-in was just swell!
origin: 1930s-1940s American English, widely used through the 1950s

definition: A woman, especially an attractive or impressive one. Common in noir culture.
example: Who's the dame in the red dress at the end of the bar?
origin: British English, popularized in American noir films of the 1940s

definition: An exclamation of surprise, wonder, or mild frustration.
example: Gee whiz, did you see the size of that new Cadillac?
origin: Euphemism for 'Jesus,' used since the 1870s, peak usage 1940s-1950s

definition: Something outstanding or the height of excellence.
example: That cherry pie your mother makes is the bee's knees.
origin: 1920s flapper slang, remained popular through the 1940s-1950s

definition: A cool, fashionable person who is aware of the latest trends, especially in jazz.
example: He's a real hep cat — knows every jazz joint in the city.
origin: Jazz culture of the 1930s-1940s, coined by Cab Calloway

definition: A boring, conventional, or uncool person who doesn't get it.
example: Don't be such a square — come out dancing with us tonight!
origin: Jazz slang from the 1940s, referring to someone out of rhythm

definition: An exclamation of approval at something or someone attractive.
example: Hubba hubba! Get a load of that new convertible!
origin: WWII military slang, possibly from Chinese 'how-pu-how'

definition: Wonderful, great, or enthusiastic about something.
example: I'm real keen on taking her to the sock hop Friday night.
origin: Old English 'cene' meaning brave; by the 1940s it meant enthusiastic or excellent

definition: An extremely attractive and desirable person.
example: That new boy who moved in down the street is a real dreamboat.
origin: 1940s-1950s American slang, popularized in teen magazines

definition: Something or someone regarded as excellent or outstanding.
example: This new television set is really the cat's meow!
origin: 1920s jazz age expression, attributed to cartoonist Tad Dorgan

definition: Getting married.
example: Did you hear? Tommy and Betty are getting hitched next month!
origin: American English from the 1800s, 'hitched' as in joining together, like horses to a wagon

definition: Looking for trouble or behaving in a way that will get you hurt.
example: Keep talking to me like that and you're cruisin' for a bruisin'.
origin: 1940s-1950s American youth slang

definition: A term of address for a man, often used in a cool or hip context.
example: What's the scene tonight, daddy-o?
origin: Bebop jazz culture of the 1940s-1950s

definition: A small child.
example: We can't go to the pictures — we've got three ankle biters to look after.
origin: 1940s colloquial English, referencing children being at ankle height

definition: In a difficult or losing position; in trouble.
example: After missing two payments, I'm really behind the eight ball.
origin: Billiards terminology from the 1930s-1940s

definition: Talking too much or saying nothing of substance.
example: Quit bumping your gums and get to work already!
origin: 1940s American slang, military usage

definition: A bald person.
example: Old chrome dome over there used to have a full head of hair.
origin: 1940s American slang comparing a bald head to shiny chrome

definition: A dollar. Used as slang for money.
example: That new suit cost me fifty clams!
origin: 1930s American slang, possibly from Native Americans using clamshells as currency

definition: Doing something really well; making excellent progress.
example: Now you're cooking with gas! That engine sounds perfect.
origin: 1940s, promoted by natural gas companies as advertising slogan

definition: A terrible dancer.
example: Don't let him near the dance floor — he's a real dead hoofer.
origin: 1940s dance hall culture; 'hoofer' was a dancer

definition: A good dancer.
example: She's a real ducky shincracker — best jitterbugger in town!
origin: 1940s swing dance culture

definition: An old-fashioned, boring, or overly conservative person.
example: Don't be such a fuddy-duddy — let the kids play their music!
origin: Early 1900s, popularized in the 1940s-1950s

definition: A woman's legs, especially attractive ones.
example: She's got a nice pair of gams, that one.
origin: Italian 'gamba' meaning leg, popularized in 1940s noir films

definition: A cigarette.
example: Got a gasper I can bum? Left mine at the office.
origin: British/American slang from WWI-WWII era

definition: Being in an exclusive romantic relationship.
example: Jimmy asked me to go steady — he even gave me his class ring!
origin: 1940s-1950s American dating culture

definition: A feeling of anxiety, nervousness, or the creeps.
example: That old abandoned house gives me the heebie-jeebies.
origin: Coined by cartoonist Billy DeBeck in the 1920s, widely used through 1940s-1950s

definition: Performing excellently; in a state of flow, especially with music.
example: The band was really in the groove last night at the dance hall.
origin: 1930s-1940s jazz slang, referencing the groove of a vinyl record

definition: An old, beat-up car.
example: He pulled up in that old jalopy making more noise than a brass band.
origin: 1920s-1930s American English, origin uncertain

definition: A famous WWII graffiti phrase and doodle, symbolizing American GI presence everywhere.
example: Every wall in Europe had 'Kilroy was here' scrawled on it.
origin: WWII American military, possibly from inspector James J. Kilroy

definition: Money, especially paper bills.
example: I'm a little short on lettuce this week — payday's Friday.
origin: 1940s American slang, green paper bills resembling lettuce leaves

definition: Having it easy; being in a comfortable, successful situation.
example: With that new promotion, you've got it made in the shade.
origin: 1940s-1950s American slang, the shade representing comfort

definition: Kissing and light romantic physical affection.
example: We caught them necking in the back row of the movie theater.
origin: 1920s American slang, widely used through the 1950s

definition: Honest, trustworthy, or legitimate.
example: Is this deal on the level, or is somebody trying to pull a fast one?
origin: Freemasonry symbolism adopted into 1940s common speech

definition: Unexpected good fortune or a lucky windfall.
example: Finding that twenty-dollar bill was like pennies from heaven.
origin: 1936 Bing Crosby song, became a popular expression through the 1940s-1950s

definition: Luxurious, fancy, or elegant. Sometimes used sarcastically.
example: Look at you in your ritzy new suit — got a hot date?
origin: From the Ritz-Carlton hotels, 1920s-1940s

definition: Go away quickly; leave immediately.
example: You kids better scram before the old man catches you!
origin: 1920s-1940s American slang, possibly from German 'schrammen'

definition: A school dance where kids danced in their socks to protect the gym floor.
example: Are you going to the sock hop after the football game?
origin: 1950s American high school culture

definition: Clothing, especially nice or stylish outfits.
example: Nice threads, daddy-o! Where'd you get that jacket?
origin: 1940s jazz culture slang

definition: Something boring, disappointing, or tedious.
example: Homework on a Saturday? What a drag!
origin: 1940s-1950s American slang

definition: Fun, excitement, or celebration. Also a euphemism for romantic activity.
example: Let's make whoopee tonight — the band's playing at the dancehall!
origin: 1920s-1940s, popularized by Eddie Cantor's song 'Makin' Whoopee'
